questions.question: Define clustering in machine learning and distinguish between hierarchical and partitional clustering approaches.
questions.hint: Focus on the fundamental difference: flat vs hierarchical structure
keyPoints: Clustering is an unsupervised learning technique that groups similar data points
Partitional clustering divides data into non-overlapping clusters
Hierarchical clustering creates a tree-like hierarchy of clusters
Partitional methods require specifying number of clusters beforehand
Hierarchical methods do not require pre-specifying cluster count

questions.question: Explain the K-means clustering algorithm with its initialization and convergence criteria.
questions.hint: Remember the iterative nature of the algorithm
keyPoints: Initialize K centroids randomly or using K-means++
Assign each data point to nearest centroid using Euclidean distance
Recalculate centroids as mean of assigned points
Repeat until convergence (centroids unchanged or change below threshold)
Objective: minimize within-cluster sum of squares (WCSS)

questions.question: Compare K-means and K-medoids clustering algorithms, highlighting their differences.
keyPoints: K-means uses centroids (mean of points) as cluster representatives
K-medoids uses medoids (actual data points) as representatives
K-medoids is more robust to outliers than K-means
K-medoids is computationally more expensive
K-means is sensitive to initialization; K-medoids is more stable

questions.question: Explain DBSCAN algorithm with the concepts of core points, border points, and noise points.
keyPoints: DBSCAN is a density-based clustering algorithm
Core points have at least minPts neighbors within epsilon distance
Border points are reachable from core points but not core points themselves
Noise points are neither core nor border points
Epsilon defines the neighborhood radius

questions.question: Describe the grid-based clustering approach and mention its advantages.
keyPoints: Grid-based clustering partitions the data space into grid cells
Statistical information is stored in each cell (cell attributes)
Cells with similar attributes form clusters
Advantages: fast processing time, independent of data order
Examples: STING, CLIQUE algorithms

questions.question: Define the term 'centroid' and 'medoid' in clustering algorithms.
questions.hint: Think about mathematical mean vs actual point
keyPoints: Centroid is the mean position of all data points in a cluster
Medoid is the most representative actual data point in a cluster
Centroid may not correspond to an actual data point
Medoid always corresponds to an existing data point
Both represent the center of a cluster in different ways

questions.question: Explain the K-means clustering algorithm in detail. Discuss the steps involved, initialization methods, and explain how to determine the optimal number of clusters using the Elbow method.
questions.hint: Include both algorithmic steps and evaluation method
keyPoints: Step 1: Initialize K centroids (random or K-means++)
Step 2: Assign each point to nearest centroid using distance metric
Step 3: Update centroids as mean of assigned points
Step 4: Check for convergence (centroid change < threshold)
Elbow method: Plot WCSS vs number of clusters
Elbow point indicates optimal K where decrease in WCSS slows down
Repeat steps 2-4 until convergence

questions.question: Discuss density-based clustering methods. Explain DBSCAN algorithm with its parameters (epsilon and minPts) and compare it with K-means clustering.
questions.hint: Focus on density connectivity and parameter selection
keyPoints: Density-based clustering finds clusters of arbitrary shape
DBSCAN uses two parameters: epsilon (neighborhood radius) and minPts (minimum points)
Core points, border points, and noise points definitions
Cluster expansion through density-reachable points
Comparison with K-means: arbitrary shapes vs spherical
DBSCAN handles outliers automatically
K-means requires predefined K; DBSCAN does not

questions.question: Explain hierarchical clustering with its two approaches: agglomerative and divisive. Discuss the different linkage methods used in hierarchical clustering.
keyPoints: Hierarchical clustering creates a dendrogram (tree structure)
Agglomerative: Start with each point as cluster, merge iteratively
Divisive: Start with one cluster, split iteratively
Single linkage: distance between nearest points of two clusters
Complete linkage: distance between farthest points
Average linkage: mean distance between all pairs
Ward's method: minimize increase in total variance

questions.question: Discuss the challenges in clustering and explain how to evaluate the quality of clustering. Mention at least two validity indices.
keyPoints: Challenges: determining optimal K, handling high-dimensional data, sensitivity to initialization, outlier handling
Internal validation: uses intrinsic information (Silhouette, Davies-Bouldin)
Silhouette coefficient: measures how similar point is to its cluster vs other clusters
Davies-Bouldin index: ratio of within-cluster scatter to between-cluster separation
External validation: compares with known ground truth (purity, NMI)
Lower silhouette score indicates poor clustering

questions.question: Explain the STING (Statistical Information Grid) algorithm for grid-based clustering. Discuss its structure and processing approach.
questions.hint: Focus on the multi-resolution grid structure
keyPoints: STING partitions data space into rectangular grid cells
Each cell stores statistical parameters (mean, std dev, count, distribution)
Hierarchical structure: different levels of granularity
Query-independent: statistical information pre-computed
Processing: Start from top level, filter unlikely cells
Proceed to lower levels for relevant cells
Advantage: fast query processing, independent of data order

questions.question: Consider the following 1D data points: {2, 3, 6, 8, 10, 14, 18}. Apply K-means algorithm with K=2. Show the cluster assignments after the first iteration if initial centroids are 3 and 10.
keyPoints: Assign points to nearest centroid: Points {2,3,6} to centroid 3; Points {8,10,14,18} to centroid 10
Recalculate new centroids: Centroid 1 = (2+3+6)/3 = 3.67; Centroid 2 = (8+10+14+18)/4 = 12.5

questions.question: Given a dataset with 4 points: A(1,2), B(2,3), C(8,9), D(9,10). Apply K-means with K=2 and initial centroids at A(1,2) and C(8,9). Calculate Euclidean distances and show the cluster assignments for first iteration.
keyPoints: Calculate distance from B(2,3) to centroids: dist to A = sqrt((2-1)²+(3-2)²) = sqrt(2) ≈ 1.41; dist to C = sqrt((2-8)²+(3-9)²) = sqrt(72) ≈ 8.49
Calculate distance from D(9,10) to centroids: dist to A = sqrt((9-1)²+(10-2)²) = sqrt(128) ≈ 11.31; dist to C = sqrt((9-8)²+(10-9)²) = sqrt(2) ≈ 1.41
Assign B to cluster 1 (centroid A), D to cluster 2 (centroid C)
Cluster 1 contains: A(1,2), B(2,3); New centroid = ((1+2)/2, (2+3)/2) = (1.5, 2.5)

questions.question: Apply DBSCAN to the following data points with epsilon=1.5 and minPts=2. Identify clusters and label noise points: P1(1,1), P2(2,1), P3(8,8), P4(9,8), P5(8,9), P6(1,3). Show your reasoning.
questions.hint: Check density connectivity for each point
keyPoints: Calculate distance matrix between all points
Points P1(1,1) and P2(2,1): distance = 1 < 1.5, so P1 and P2 are neighbors
Points P3(8,8), P4(9,8), P5(8,9): distances among them < 1.5, form dense region
Points P1, P2 each have 1 neighbor (minPts=2), so they are border points
Points P3, P4, P5 each have 2 neighbors (≥ minPts), so they are core points
Point P6(1,3): distance to P1=2, to P2=2.24, both > 1.5, so P6 is noise
Cluster 1: {P1, P2}; Cluster 2: {P3, P4, P5}; Noise: {P6}
